essays.prompt:
What excites you about a career in engineering/technology? Why do you think your abilities and skills are a good fit for
Plaksha's B.Tech program?
essays.full_text:
From a young age, I've been fascinated by how things work-whether it's the logic behind mobile apps, the science in smart devices, or the
innovation that powers everyday life. What excites me most about engineering and technology is the ability to solve real-world problems
using creativity and logic. It's not just about building machines or writing code-it's about designing solutions that can create meaningful
impact. The ever-evolving nature of tech, combined with its potential to transform industries from healthcare to climate, makes this field
incredibly exciting for me. I believe my curiosity, collaborative mindset, and eagerness to learn make me a strong fit for Plaksha's B.Tech program. I enjoy working on
projects, asking questions, and applying what I learn to practical challenges. I also have a keen interest in interdisciplinary learning-blending
tech with design, data, and human behavior-which I understand is core to Plaksha's approach. What draws me most to Plaksha is its focus
on innovation, entrepreneurship, and societal impact. I'm excited by the opportunity to learn from faculty, mentors, and peers who are
passionate about building a better future through technology. Plaksha's hands-on curriculum, values-driven mission, and emphasis on
leadership resonate deeply with my aspirations.

essays.prompt:
Share a time when you contributed to a project or activity that positively affected your school or community. What
challenges did you face, and what did you learn about leadership and perseverance?
essays.full_text:
Having grown up with a pet dog, I've developed a deep sense of empathy and care for animals. That personal connection inspired me to take
action when I noticed several stray dogs in my neighborhood struggling with hunger, injuries, and neglect. I initiated a small project with a
few classmates to support these animals by organizing feeding drives, arranging basic medical aid through local NGOs, and spreading
awareness about responsible and humane treatment of strays. One of the major challenges was addressing concerns from some residents who were initially uncomfortable with our activities. There were
worries about safety and cleanliness. Instead of giving up, I took the time to understand their perspective and created a small awareness
campaign. We shared how caring for strays through sterilization and regular feeding could actually make the community safer. We also made
sure to clean up after every feeding and involved local animal welfare volunteers to lend credibility to our efforts. This experience taught me that leadership is as much about listening and empathy as it is about action. It showed me how
perseverance-combined with teamwork and a clear sense of purpose-can gradually change mindsets and make a difference. It also
reinforced that even small initiatives, when pursued with sincerity, can have a positive and lasting impact.

themes.title: Aspirational Technologist or Practiced Builder
themes.unifying_axis: This theme concerns the relationship between the applicant's stated technology identity and their demonstrated lived practice.
themes.interview_direction: Whether the applicant's technology-facing identity is already practiced and self-directed or still primarily aspirational.

themes.title: Initiative, Ownership, and Real Agency
themes.unifying_axis: This theme concerns whether the applicant's stories of contribution reflect genuine ownership under friction rather than surface participation.
themes.interview_direction: How the applicant actually acts, decides, and adapts when responsibility becomes real.

signals.title: Tech Identity Without Visible Practice
signals.evidence_anchor: The engineering essay makes technology central to the applicant's identity and future direction.
signals.direct_read: The record contains strong technology-facing language but little visible self-directed technical building work in activities.
signals.depth_opening: Whether the applicant's technology-facing identity is already grounded in lived practice or still mostly aspirational.
signals.why_it_matters: Resolving this changes how the applicant's entire technology-facing self-presentation should be understood.

signals.title: Strong Scores, Real Reasoning Unknown
signals.evidence_anchor: The academic and test record shows very high performance, especially in mathematics.
signals.direct_read: The applicant performs strongly in structured academic problem-solving across school and JEE Mains.
signals.depth_opening: How the applicant reasons when the problem is open-ended, ambiguous, or not exam-shaped.
signals.why_it_matters: This determines whether the applicant's evident capability extends beyond structured performance settings.

signals.title: Community Action and Actual Ownership
signals.evidence_anchor: The community essay describes initiating a stray-dog support effort with classmates and responding to neighborhood resistance.
signals.direct_read: The applicant can narrate a socially grounded initiative involving persistence, persuasion, and coordination.
signals.depth_opening: How much of the decision-making and adaptation in that effort was personally owned by the applicant in practice.
signals.why_it_matters: Resolving this changes whether the story reads as real agency or mostly participant-level involvement.

questions: Your essay makes technology sound central to how you see yourself. Outside coursework, where have you actually built something technical on your own and what happened when it became difficult?

Your academic record shows very strong structured performance, especially in mathematics. What is a problem you pursued when there was no clear method or answer key, and how did you know what to try next?
Looking at your current record, what is the strongest evidence you would give that technology is something you do and not only something you want to do?
In the stray-dog effort, what was one decision you made yourself after residents pushed back, and why did you choose that response instead of another one?
When you say you initiated that effort with classmates, what part of the work would not have happened if you had stepped away?
What did that experience teach you about the difference between caring about an issue and actually taking responsibility for it when other people are uncomfortable?

deterministic_signals.observation: Leadership experience includes the role 'School House Captain' with 1.0-year duration.
Music Piano reached International level participation over 5.0 years.
Olympiads reached District level participation over 7.0 years.
